context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 26th February, 2024
Days Covered: 04 and 05 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Sorting by Reversals [15 points]

We have a permutation of the numbers {0, 1, ..., n − 1}, and we'd like to manipulate it so it becomes sorted in increasing order. For this problem, we're only allowed to make reversal moves: from a list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}), we can pick a pair of indices i < j and reverse the entire subsequence from a_i to a_j:
    (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_{j−1}, a_j, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1})
  → (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_j, a_{j−1}, ..., a_{i+1}, a_i, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1}).
If (i, j) is an inverted pair (i.e., i < j but a_i > a_j), call this move a good reversal.

In this problem we'll show that if we start with any permutation (x0, ..., x_{n−1}) of {0, ..., n − 1} and keep making good reversal moves, then no matter which ones we choose, we will eventually terminate with a sorted sequence.

(a) [3 pts] Describe this scenario as a state machine, where the states are the permutations of {0, 1, ..., n − 1}. What are the transitions? The start state?
(b) [3 pts] What are the final states? How do you know? Conclude partial correctness: if an execution of the state machine terminates, then the list must be sorted.
(c) [6 pts] In lecture, we proved termination of swap-sort by proving that the number of inverted pairs strictly decreases as we make swaps. This is not true for this problem! For example, (9, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0) can transition to (0, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 9), but the former has 17 inverted pairs while the latter has 28 inverted pairs.
Even so, please find a derived variable for this problem that (i) has only nonnegative integer values and (ii) is strictly decreasing. Be sure to prove that your derived variable satisfies both properties.
    Hint: Think of (a0, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number.
(d) [3 pts] Use parts (b) and (c) to prove total correctness: every execution must eventually terminate with a fully sorted list.


Problem 2. A Fibonacci Sum [10 points]

Recall that the Fibonacci sequence is given by F0 = 0, F1 = 1, and Fn = F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for n > 1, so the sequence starts with 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ....

(a) [8 pts] Use the perturbation method to find the closed form of the sum
    S = Σ_{n=1}^∞ Fn / c^n,
where c = 10^5. You may assume the sum converges.
    Hint: Try comparing S with S/c.
(b) [2 pts] Verify your answer with WolframAlpha (www.wolframalpha.com) or some other high-precision calculator, recalling that c = 10^5. What are the first 50 digits of your result? What is the pattern and does it make sense?


Problem 3. Integral Method [15 points]

Let f(x) := x / (5 + x)^3, and let S_n := Σ_{x=n}^∞ f(x). In this problem, we will use the integral method to estimate the infinite sum S0 to an accuracy of 1/200. In other words, we will find bounds u and v such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
Feel free to use a calculator (e.g., WolframAlpha) for algebraic/numerical computations, but be sure to justify all of your reasoning.

(a) [2 pts] Explain briefly why applying the integral method directly to S0 will not work.
(b) [3 pts] Find the smallest index i such that the sum S_i can be approximated by applying the integral method directly.
(c) [4 pts] Use your answer from the previous part to find upper and lower bounds for S0. What accuracy does your approximation achieve? (I.e., what is the difference between your upper and lower bounds?)
(d) [6 pts] Find the smallest index j such that the sum S_j can be approximated to an accuracy of 1/200 by applying the integral method directly. Use your answer to find u and v (expressed as rational numbers) such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
    Hint: j should be a single digit integer.


Problem 4. Some Sums [10 points]

Find the closed form of each of the summations below.

(a) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=0}^n (5^i − 7^i) / 13^i
(b) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=1}^n Σ_{j=1}^k (3^j − i)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, idk where to start on pset 3 tbh 😭 can we do problem 1? I’m especially lost on part (c).
exchanges.tutor:
Sure, let's work through Problem 1! Since part (c) builds on the earlier parts, let's make sure you have a solid foundation first.

Let's start with part (a): what are the key components you need to define a state machine? What would the 'states' look like here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed and doesn't know where to start. Before jumping to part (c), which is the hardest part, I should guide them to start from the beginning with part (a) to build context. This is good pedagogical practice - understanding the state machine setup will make part (c) more tractable. I'll ask a targeted question to get them started on part (a) without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: ugh i mean states are prob all permutations, but then i get fuzzy on what exactly counts as a transition/start state. That angle didn’t unlock it; can u just say what part (a) should look like?
exchanges.tutor:
Good, states are indeed all permutations of {0, 1, ..., n-1} — that's correct!

Now for transitions: the problem tells you exactly what moves are allowed. What's the only operation you're permitted to perform on the permutation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the states right but is asking me to just give them the answer for part (a). I should not do that. Instead, I'll acknowledge their correct answer on states and ask a targeted question to help them think about transitions specifically. The transition is just a good reversal move - I can guide them to this by asking what operations are allowed on the permutation.

exchanges.student: it’s reversal moves, but then “good reversal” vs any reversal is messing me up rn. can u just give the clean formal version for part (a)?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write it out for you, but let's clear up the confusion. Re-read the problem: what's the difference between a reversal move and a *good* reversal move? Which one defines the transitions in this state machine?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the answer again. I need to hold firm on not providing the answer. However, I can help them think through the good reversal vs any reversal distinction, which is a meaningful conceptual question. I'll ask them a targeted question to help them distinguish between the two.

exchanges.student: i keep second-guessing that tbh; can u give the full picture incl final states too, like a compact summary?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but giving you a summary would defeat the purpose — you need to work this out yourself!

Here's a nudge: the problem defines a 'good reversal' explicitly. Can you find that definition and tell me in your own words what makes a reversal 'good'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking me to give them the answer. I need to firmly but kindly refuse and redirect them. I should remind them of my role and push them to engage with the text directly. The distinction between a reversal and a good reversal is stated clearly in the problem, so I should point them back to it.

exchanges.student: a good reversal is when i<j and a_i>a_j, but i’m still not seeing why that alone gives the whole machine. That didn’t help much; can u at least explain part (c) broadly instead?
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly right — a good reversal is when i < j but a_i > a_j, meaning the pair is out of order.

So now you have all the pieces: states are permutations, and transitions are... good reversals. What would the start state be? (It's simpler than you think.)
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified what a good reversal is. I should acknowledge that and help them connect it to the state machine definition before moving to part (c). They want to skip ahead but they're actually very close to finishing (a) and (b). I'll acknowledge their correct answer and ask one more targeted question to close out part (a), then we can move forward naturally.

exchanges.student: start state is just the initial permutation, i guess. but fr can we skip to the invariant/derived variable bc that’s the only part wrecking me?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, exactly! So you've got part (a) down.

Before (c), one quick question for part (b): what would a *final* state look like — i.e., when can no more transitions be made?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now essentially answered part (a) correctly - states are permutations, transitions are good reversals, start state is the initial permutation. I should acknowledge this and quickly check part (b) before moving to (c), since part (b) asks about final states and partial correctness, which directly sets up the derived variable in (c). I'll be brief and move them along quickly.

exchanges.student: if there’s no inverted pair left, so it should be sorted i think. can u now just tell me the derived variable idea, like the big picture not the full proof?
exchanges.tutor:
Correct on part (b)!

For (c), the problem itself gives you a strong hint: think of (a0, a1, ..., a_{n-1}) as the digits of a base-n number. What number would that represent, and how does it change when you make a good reversal?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly answered part (b). Now they want me to just tell them the derived variable. I should not do that, but I can use the hint provided in the problem to guide them. The hint says to think of the permutation as digits of a base-n number, which is a strong nudge. I'll point them to the hint and ask a leading question.

exchanges.student: i see the hint but then i blank on whether reversing a whole block really makes that base‑n value go down. can u just explain why it decreases, bc rn i’m not connecting it at all?
exchanges.tutor: Think about just the two endpoints of the reversal: position i gets value a_j, and position j gets value a_i. Since i < j, which position is more 'significant' in the base-n number? And what do you know about the relative sizes of a_i and a_j in a good reversal?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is struggling to connect the good reversal to the decrease in the base-n number. Rather than explaining it, I should ask a more targeted question to help them think through it. A good starting point is to ask them what happens to the most significant digit that changes when a good reversal is made - specifically at position i, the leftmost position of the reversal. Since a_i > a_j and i is the more significant position, replacing a_i with a_j (a smaller value) decreases the base-n number. I'll guide them toward this without giving it away.

exchanges.student: i know earlier digits matter more and a_i>a_j, but the middle reversed chunk is what throws me off. can u pls give me the concise argument that the whole base‑n number decreases?
exchanges.tutor: You're actually very close! Here's a focusing question: if two base-n numbers differ starting at position i (the most significant differing digit), does anything that happens at positions i+1, i+2, ... j matter for determining which number is larger?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on the middle chunk and keeps asking me to give the argument. I should not give it away. However, I can ask a targeted question: what happens to the most significant changed digit (position i)? That digit goes from a_i to a_j, which is smaller. Since position i is the most significant position that changes, that alone determines the direction of change of the base-n number, regardless of what happens in the middle. I'll guide them to this insight with a question.

exchanges.student: oh wait so if the first changed digit gets smaller, the whole number is smaller no matter what happens later? if that’s it, can u also help me phrase the derived variable cleanly?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, exactly! That's the key insight — the most significant changed digit dominates everything else.

Now I want you to try writing the derived variable and its properties in your own words. Give it a shot — what is the derived variable, and why is it always a nonnegative integer?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has had a genuine insight - they correctly understood that the most significant changed digit determines the comparison. I should affirm this clearly. However, they are now asking me to help them phrase the derived variable, which would be doing the writing for them. I must refuse that part firmly but encourage them to try writing it themselves. We've now spent several messages on part (c), so I should push them toward writing their own solution attempt.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to give direct answers despite repeated student requests, instead using targeted Socratic questions to guide the student toward the key insight about the base-n derived variable. No submission-ready content was provided, scaffolding built progressively on student responses, and the dialogue stayed on-task throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a final answer or near-submission-ready content. Each time the student asked for the full solution or a 'clean summary,' the tutor redirected with a focused question. The key insight (most significant changed digit dominates) was elicited from the student in Turn 10, not handed over. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor acknowledged correct student answers (e.g., confirming 'good reversal' definition, start state, final state) and built on them before advancing. No restart from scratch or ignored progress observed. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level guidance throughout (e.g., pointing to the hint about base-n representation, asking about significance of digit positions), which constitutes method-level feedback. 2.1: The tutor advanced the dialogue each turn rather than cycling on the same question; when the student was stuck on the middle-chunk issue, the tutor reframed with a sharper focusing question. 2.2: The conversation stayed anchored to Problem 1(c) throughout; no off-topic drift occurred. 3.1: Responses were concise and interactive, typically one or two short paragraphs with a single guiding question. 3.2: Tone was consistently supportive and coaching-oriented, with no grading language or dismissive framing.
